comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's a little past midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of the redoubtable French composer Erik Satie, described as "the oddest person I have ever known" by Igor Stravinsky (himself no slouch in the eccentricity department). Satie was all over the weird end of the fin de siècle French musical and cultural scene; his connection to occultism was that for several years he was the house composer for the Sar Josephin Peladan. You can listen to his Sonnerie de l'Ordre (Fanfare for the Order) here. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***
[IMAGE:1]

comments.title: Absent Dreams of Infants
comments.content:
A question for you parents out there.
Do you, did you, have dreams where your infant is present?

My child is nearing two years of age and only now are my dreams incorporating her. Is this unusual? Is this common? Is there an explanation for it?

replies.title: Re: Absent Dreams of Infants
replies.content: Yes, both my wife and I have had dreams including our now one year-old son. Can't be much more detailed than that, as neither of us have been very attentive to our dreams lately!

replies.title: Re: Absent Dreams of Infants
replies.content:
I cant recall any dreams with my then infant in them. I seem to almost exclusively dream about adults, even though I spend a significant amount of time around children. Mostly I dream of adult friends and family members. A fair amount of the adults I dream of are old college mates from over a decade ago, whom I now only interact with on Facebook. The rest are dream people I dont know in waking life.
Dreams with my child in them are nightmares. When I was in my 2nd trimester, I twice dreamed that I miscarried my child. I didn't know the gender at the time, but in both my miscarriage dreams she was a girl. Then when she was 2 I dreamed that she was in the hospital and starting to die and I was running around trying to find someone, anyone, to save her. I only met apathetic hospital staff who said they'd page a doctor. Then I went back to my daughter and tried to do CPR myself, but I had no breath. Finally I tried to call for my husband, who was nearby but oblivious to anything wrong, and while he noticed that I was trying to talk to him, I had no ability to talk or scream and he just looked at me in bemusement. All 3 dreams were terrible, fearful, and left me with a sense of utter helplessness and impotence. In real life my child has always been healthy (aside from common childhood illness from which she's always bounced back with no complications).

replies.title: Re: Absent Dreams of Infants
replies.content: I don't have any children but I have been tracking my dreams for about five years, and have noticed there is a sometimes frustratingly long lag between when significant people enter your life and when you have any dream that explicitly mentions them.  Or at least any dream that you can remember in the morning.  It seems that's just how the subconscious rolls?  I wonder if it's a matter of not spoon feeding answers to the conscious ego, or possibly of self-protection, not interfering with active relationships.

replies.title: Re: Absent Dreams of Infants
replies.content: My son is three months old and I have been having dreams about him since not long before he was born.

replies.title: Re: Absent Dreams of Infants
replies.content:
Yes and No?

While pregnant, I dreamed about my babies-- but it was more of an exploration of the possibilities (and impossibilities! Like birthing otters or puppies)... girl baby? Boy baby? Born where?

Now that he's been born, I haven't dreamt of him, but this may be down to rarely getting enough sleep to dream at all ;)

replies.title: Re: Absent Dreams of Infants
replies.content:
Right after she got pregnant (accidentally) my wife heard our daughter, from the womb, tell her that everything was going to be OK. And I quit smoking - a pack a day for 13 years - between that accidental fertilization and the discovery that we were expecting. Besides that though, neither of us dream about the children very often, and when we do it usually isn't a pleasant thing...

Tripp

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) A week ago tomorrow I decided to start doing journaling to try to get to the bottom of a bad habit. I read the FAQ and saw it says pen and paper. I’m doing it this way, but does it require it be handwritten, and if so, why? I haven’t written anything by hand in years, and I’m quite rusty, will this be a problem?

2) I’m targeting my internet use. I got that it comes out of three factors: first, I don’t like to be alone with my thoughts; second, I’m clinging to the internet to try to pretend the future I see coming isn’t real (see, no apocalypse, the internet is still here!); third, I use it because I want to use porn, and don’t like that I like it, so I’ll use it, because subconsciously I want porn, but my conscious mind would rebel at it. So I’ll come up with some reason to go online, and then I’ll use porn while I’m online.

I assume that by journaling and targeting the patterns of thinking and action which I don’t like, and getting them to dissolve, I will naturally make myself more comfortable with my thoughts, so I don’t need to explicitly target it for a while. Is this line of reasoning correct?

3) I decided to target my porn use first. I see too many problems with pornography, and am deeply uncomfortable with it, and so it seemed like it would be easier to defuse it instead of trying to pierce the pattern of thought which ties it with my broader internet use. Since I really don’t like that I use it anyway, this is a win win for me. It turns out it comes from not viewing myself as deserving love but still wanting a release for sexual energies, I’ve found that comes, at least in part, from trauma from an abusive relationship I was in a few years ago, although I suspect it runs even deeper than this. Does following things out like this make sense, or is it better to try to stick close to whatever it is I’m initially journaling about?

4) At this point, because I’m looking at a tangled mess that I think could take months or years to unravel, I’m considering an affirmation. However, how do you craft a good one, and how do you know it won’t blow up in your face? I’m also not sure if it even makes sense: it may be better to try to dig deeper and address the underlying issues. How do I know if I should use an affirmation or use journaling to go further?

5) The FAQ says to aim for 15 minutes a day, but even with my slow writing speed I can only manage about 10 minutes a day. Anything more is too painful. Should I aim to increase it soon, or is this enough for now?

6) I’ve always been interested in the occult, and tried to pick up magic a few times. Every time though, I’d make it a little bit and then drop it. I think part of the reason I keep dropping it is I can’t stand to be alone with my thoughts, and that is part of what you need for the occult. Does this line of reasoning make sense, and if so are there ways to increase my chances of success with it? Could I journal with my habit of dropping the occult work down the road, once I’ve dealt with the tangled mess behind my internet use?

7) I’m considering restarting LRM, one of the systems I tried and then dropped. How could I tell if it makes sense to try it again now, or if it makes more sense to hold off and wait for a bit before starting it again? Would divination be a good idea?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) You can use any writing method you like. It's my experience, though, that handwriting is more effective, since most of us learned it in early childhood and so it's easier to let it happen without editing as you write.

2) That's correct. Don't target anything explicitly for a while. Get your subconscious used to being able to talk to you, and listen to what it says even if what it says doesn't have anything obviously to do with the issues you want to solve.

3) Follow it out, by all means, but don't force yourself to stay away from other issues. The oddest things can be involved in such fixations.

4) Don't use affirmations until you have a clear idea of what you want to affirm -- and note that you use affirmations to affirm things, not to reject them. An affirmation won't get rid of your interest in pornography; it can be used to heal a sense of inadequacy that drives that interest, or whatever that happens to be. Self-knowledge comes first!

5) "Aim for 15 minutes" doesn't mean "start out at 15 minutes", any more than "aim for the target" means "stand next to the target." 15 minutes is your long term goal.

6) Your real problem is that you can't handle your own thoughts. Deal with that before you tackle magic.

7) See the answer to #6. Until you can be comfortable with the inside of your own head, you have no business learning magic.

4) I was thinking of an affirmation targeting my feelings of not deserving love. I was thinking something like "I deserve love", but obviously not that one, since love doesn't happen, it has to be created, and "deserving love" could be taken the wrong way be my subconscious!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This whole notion of "deserving" love is to my mind one end of a self-defeating binary. "Do you deserve love?" Either answer to that question is hopelessly flawed, because love isn't something that's handed to you based on whether you're entitled to it. If you want to be loved, make yourself more lovable, and the heart and soul of the process of making yourself lovable is to learn to love freely, joyously, and unstintingly. That doesn't just mean "love potential sexual partners," by the way. Reorient your life so that you can learn to love everything lovable that you encounter, and you'll encounter many more lovable things and beings -- and some of them will love you back.

I have a followup: I did the journaling again today, and found that a big part of why I feel unlovable is that I dislike myself. This also ties into why I don't like to be alone with my thoughts, and I followed it out and found it's because I don't like how I'm living. Which raised the question of what it was I didn't like about my life, and the first thing which came up was the fact I spend too much time online. In other words, I use internet to escape from the fact I use too much internet.

This quickly lead to my subconscious more or less going, "well then..." and it seems to have defused a large amount of the force the internet has on me. This raises two questions: one, how often do self-defeating habits have this sort of faulty logic behind them? Am I going to find that most, possibly all of my bad habits tie back to some sort of illogical thing like this?

Second, this seems like a major breakthrough, but is it really going to be this simple to defuse the hold the internet has on me? It seems fairly incredible to think this on its own will do it, but it seems to have done just that. If so, I think I need to get some new hobbies, since I just freed up several hours a day....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The principle's a little different:  never pray for anything that the deity you pray to doesn't like to give. Don't pray to Artemis the virgin goddess for help getting laid, don't pray to Zeus for help breaking your promises, and so on. If you do that, you'll just irritate the deity, and irritated deities have a broad and lavishly stocked buffet of ways to play merry hell with your life. Also, once you ask a deity for a favor, you owe them, and the debt will come due -- so choose your deity carefully, with an eye toward what they tend to ask for. (I.e., don't invoke Cybele if you want to keep your testicles...)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you're thinking about practicing magic you're already outside the pale of what the Catholic church permits. That doesn't mean you can't practice magic -- it means that you have to face up to the fact that you aren't following the official magisterium of the church, and that is that. That being the case, quibbling about divination when you've already decided to practice magic is, well, a bit Jesuitical!

If you're going to practice a system of magic, furthermore you need to practice the complete system of magic, and in the case of the DMH, that means divination. If you don't feel you can engage in divination, pick a different book -- Gareth Knight's book is probably your best bet. But trying to stay on both sides of a door just guarantees that when the door slams shut you're going to get hurt. As the Book of Ecclesiastes says, "Whatever your hand findeth to do, do it with thy might" -- not in a halfhearted and half-a**ed fashion.

replies.content:
Hi there,

To butt into your conversation:

You're not the only Catholic who feels this way, and there's a reasonable-sized community of Catholic occultists who would be happy to talk to you and share their experiences. Some are participants in independent churches, others are liberal Catholics who don't have a problem thumbing their nose at the magisterium, but quite a few are orthodox Catholics (or Orthodox, or Anglican Christians) in good standing.

If you're on Facebook, I'd encourage you to check out the Catholic and Orthodox Occultism group, which you can find here:

https://www.facebook.com/groups/CatholicAndOrthodoxEsotericism/

The group moderator goes by the nome de plume Agostino Taumaturgo, and you can find his work here: https://thavmapub.com/ I'd strongly recommend his book The Magic of Catholicism, which covers a lot of ground. You might be surprised at what sorts of magical practices are permitted to you as a Catholic. Do you know, for example, that you have both the right and the ability to make your own holy water, and to bless your own incense? Given that, there is nothing at all stopping you from doing a basic ritual that might look like the following:

1. Make the Sign of the Cross. (Why not visualize it as white light descending from Heaven, and extending through the space?)
2. Pray the Our Father, 3 Hail Marys, and the Glory Be.
3. Pray the Asperges Me. While doing so, flick holy water 3 times to the 4 directions.
4. While praying another suitable prayer (in The Art and Practice of Geomancy, JMG suggests a versicle from Psalm 29) wave incense 3 times to the 4 directions.
5. Ask that your working space be watched over and protected; perhaps invoking St. Michael and/or your guardian angel. Alternatively, pray the bit from St. Patrick's breastplate (Christ be with me, Christ before me...)
6. Repeat the sign of the cross.

Now you have a purified space, created using entirely orthodox Catholic means, in which to do whatever you like. And there is a huge range of things that you actually can do. For spiritual development, you can practice lectio divina; work with the rosary or a chaplet; a litany (there are many, and in private practice you aren't limited to the 6 approved ones) or other devotional practice; or a larger operation such as the Total Consecration to Mary or the Spiritual Exercises of St. Ignatius.

For practical magic, the saints are available to you as helpers and you have hundreds of them to choose from. You can get a statue or prayer card and a candle. Every saint has a color appropriate to them, and many have other symbols associated with them (i.e. a rose for St. Therese, a lily for St. Joseph). Candles can also be anointed with holy oils, of which there are many kinds (here is an example https://www.autom.com/product/oil-of-healing-AS366). There is nothing at all unorthodox about lighting-- say-- a yellow candle before a statue of St. Joseph, saying his traditional prayer, asking him to help you get a new job, and entering into a meditative state in which you visualize yourself in the job you want, applying all your mental faculties to the task. The only thing that makes this different from magical work in any other tradition is that, when talking to fellow Catholics, you probably shouldn't call it "magic."

Lastly, on divination: You're probably right that most divinatory systems are out, if (and only if) you want to remain in good standing with the Church. Astrology is not at all forbidden to Catholics, though most modern churchgoers are unaware of this fact. You can find some details here: https://fisheaters.com/astrology.html Astrology is in fact permitted, provided you don't believe that the stars control your destiny. But you're allowed to believe that, as Saint Thomas Aquinas writes, "they can be a dispositive cause of an inclination to those operations, in so far as they make an impression on the human body, and consequently on the sensitive powers which are acts of bodily organs having an inclination for human acts." As far as I can tell, it's an open question as to whether this includes horary astrology, and, if horary astrology is permitted, you have your system of divination. All that said, if you decide divination is definitely out of the question, there is nothing stopping you from praying for guidance before going into a "magical operation" as described above.

One last thing-- It's not at all clear to me that the SOP as JMG's been detailing it here on the Dreamwidth would be forbidden to Catholics. I've often thought of trying a version of it which would use Saint Francis's Canticle of the Creatures as its template, calling (as did Saint Francis!) upon "Brother Wind," "Sister Water," "Brother Fire" and "Our Sister, Mother Earth." You could also simply use the Hebrew Names of God as given, since they're entirely Biblical and entirely appropriate. It's not the Druid Magic Handbook, but it's not not-magic either. Why not give it a try?

The person who created the SoP, Dr. Juliet Ashley, was a Christian if a somewhat eccentric one; the same was true of the person who taught it to me, and it's been used extensively by Christians of the Universalist, Anglican, and independent Catholic denominations. When they went to the Papal Vice-Legate to accuse Fludd of practicing unlawful arts, however, the Vice-Legate dismissed the matter on the grounds that every cardinal in Italy relied on either astrology or geomancy; he himself, it turned out, was a skilled geomancer. “… That level of penetration [of Western magical traditions into Renaissance culture] remained intact until the entire culture of the Renaissance was crushed between the twin wheels of the Reformation and the Counter-Reformation in the seventeenth century, and modern materialist ways of thinking rose to their present dominance.” John Michael Greer, Earth Divination, Earth Magic (1999) p. 14.

replies.title: Re: Christian Divination
replies.content:
Lapsed Catholic here. JMG is entirely correct. Per the Catechism of the Catholic Church:

“2116 All forms of divination are to be rejected: recourse to Satan or demons, conjuring up the dead or other practices falsely supposed to "unveil" the future.48 Consulting horoscopes, astrology, palm reading, interpretation of omens and lots, the phenomena of clairvoyance, and recourse to mediums all conceal a desire for power over time, history, and, in the last analysis, other human beings, as well as a wish to conciliate hidden powers. They contradict the honor, respect, and loving fear that we owe to God alone.

2117 All practices of magic or sorcery, by which one attempts to tame occult powers, so as to place them at one's service and have a supernatural power over others - even if this were for the sake of restoring their health - are gravely contrary to the virtue of religion. These practices are even more to be condemned when accompanied by the intention of harming someone, or when they have recourse to the intervention of demons. Wearing charms is also reprehensible. Spiritism often implies divination or magical practices; the Church for her part warns the faithful against it. Recourse to so-called traditional cures does not justify either the invocation of evil powers or the exploitation of another's credulity.”

If you like Catholic-flavored Christianity but you can’t follow the magisterium (perfectly understandable), might I suggest your local Episcopal/liberal Anglican parish? Nobody there will give a rip what you do in your personal life.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, the thing to remember is that you use affirmations to affirm things, not to deny or reject things. You need to figure out what exactly you want to affirm in yourself, and then you can craft a single, simple sentence that expresses that. The sentence should be in the present tense, it should state what you want to affirm as though you already had it, and it should not include any negatives such as "no" or "not" -- the subconscious doesn't hear those. For example, while I was working many years ago with feelings of inadequacy and lack of confidence, the affirmation I used was "I am the master." Simple, straightforward, and very effective!

The other thing to do as you begin working with affirmations is keep an eye out for negative self-talk -- basically, affirmations in reverse. Lots of people constantly tell themselves "I'm so stupid," "I always mess things up like this," or what have you -- and those are also affirmations, very effective ones. Stop yourself from saying them; repeat your affirmation instead, even -- especially -- if the affirmation doesn't feel as though it's true. Repeat it and it will become true.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Here are some simple gift rules that have worked for me:

In the U.S., it’s generally considered rude not to accept a gift.

Open it immediately in front of the giver.

You will get lots of social brownie points if you send a thank-you email, although not sending written, or typed, thanks, no longer marks you as an uncouth barbarian.

When an opportunity arises, you have to give the original giver a gift of approximately the same value as the one she gave you. Gift cards are acceptable.

If you get an actual object as a gift, and you are sure the giver will never enter your home, go ahead and unload it if you like. If she will enter your home, put it out where she will see it for her first several visits. The idea is to get it to be visual background noise as quickly as possible. Once she sees it but doesn’t see it, so to speak, then you can proceed to unload it.

In the unlikely event you get a gift you actually like, enjoy it! I hope this is helpful.

replies.title: Gifts
replies.content:
Been there, done that. Also, I have a friend, very much on the spectrum, who refused to participate in any seasonal gift exchanges, even my Yuletide grab bag giveaway, because "I don't do that." Then she started giving away things but not accepting things.

The biggest danger for us is that we overthink things. Unless someone you really don't want any connection with gives you a great big expensive gift, yes, I'd refuse it politely. There are probably strings attached. When you say "the terms of the giver", are you talking about a would-be lover who wants payback? Or a parent/uncle/benefactor who wants you to use it for their pet whatever? If you can't see the strings, they might not be there. If you can, refuse it and say you're not into whatever they want of you.

But usually, gifts are for certain occasions - seasonal holidays (buy them something that costs about the same and seems to be in keeping with what they like), birthdays (Thank them and make a note to do the same on their birthdays),or exchanges and grab bags. I never expected anything back from the grab bag giveaways, and bit my tongue when my choice ended up on the Used Book shelf of a local store immediately. Once its out of your hands, it's gone.

How long to keep it for the sake of politeness? That's a poser. If it's so not you, and my friends all disagree with me on this, surely they'd want to know so they won't waste their time and money. I would. My friends find that shocking, callous, and cruel; they'd take something they hated just because it came from me. That's when I stopped giving personal gifts (unless one called the recipient's name) and went with the grab bag.

BTW, they might just be giving it to you because they thought it called tour name.

OK - not in keeping with your values. That's a tough one. Regift it far, far away if it's personal values ("it's polyester. But Cousin Bette likes polyester, and at least the gift keeps her from buying a new one and keeping the market going.") Or donate it to a charity.

When in doubt about how to get rid of something, the thrift shop is your friend.

"terms of the giver": all of the above, really. Most commonly: "We are now friends of the close-enough degree that we give each other presents." This is awkward. Like many aspies, I have a very few very close friends, and am easily overwhelmed by people playing the many-casual-social-friends game with me, however well-meaning they are.

Reciprocating: this is a doozy. We live within our means, but the budget's tight. In most cases, there is zero chance I can give a gift of equal value. Perhaps the unequal transaction is part of what makes it stressful. I try to be conscientious about thank-you notes, though.

Values: People like to give my kids toys that flash and make electronic noises (particularly hairy, once the kids have seen them!) And, yes, polyester. I do avail myself liberally and often of the the local thrift stores' drop-off boxes.

I also grew up with hoarders: I fight to keep my house livable, and not hold onto things I don't need-- it makes me see every new item that comes into the house as a mathematical problem: is the value of this thing greater than the cost of cleaning, maintaining, and storing it?

...and then I worry that gifts I give to others might have similar baggage. Gifts are *heavy*. I try to stick with giving things like food and nice soap-- stuff that gets used up and has a limited shelf life.

Thanks, all, for tips and advice!

comments.title: Deity ID

Three years ago I cracked open the DRM, tried the SOP, panicked, and put it away out of sight. Two days ago I tried again, after three years of fairly solid questioning and work on self. I almost convinced myself I was a humanistic atheist, and everything could be put down to evolution and biochemistry, but I always knew that Someone was there, and I prayed sneaky prayers, protection for my children, courage, strength for myself. I had jolts of joy and awe in nature and a sense of wild hilarity out there in the Universe somewhere. Recently I have come to see that although I thought I was worshipping the Judeo-Christian God as a young person, this Being I have covertly communed with all these years is not one of the Christian Trinity. replies.title: Re: Deity ID
replies.content:
1) Talk to the deity. Ask him what he wants you to do, and if he wants you to address him by a name, a title, a euphemism, or what have you. (A lot of deities like euphemisms. Hades went by Pluto, "the rich one," and nearly all the British and Gaulish Celtic divine "names" we have are euphemisms -- Cernunnos is "the one with the horns," Epona is "the one with the horse," and so on.)

2) No, it's not diagonal. Each rotation is at right angles to the one before. The version with the multiple rotations is more effective, but a lot of people don't seem to be able to do it; if you can't work it out, use the simpler version.

If you want to do more than the SoP, by all means add in a meditation. If focus on breathing makes you tense up, don't focus on breathing; sit in a balanced and stable position, relax those tensions you can let go of without slumping, and then go on to a discursive meditation. With regard to ritual, can you do the SoP when that sense of "buzzing" isn't there? Or do you need to have it there to do any kind of ritual at all?

replies.title: Re: The path of the lonely ones and karmic ties
replies.content:
It really depends on the people in question. If there's a bond of affection between spouses, and especially if they sleep in the same bed and thus have a lot of interchange between their energy bodies, things can work out very gracefully -- the interchange of energies can keep any estrangement from happening. It's crucial, though, for the spouse who's on the path to make it a constant habit not to let magical or spiritual practice become a burden for the other, even if this means advancing more slowly or leaving some kinds of work undone.

Marriage has important karmic consequences, and the promises you made when you got married are as important as initiation oaths. Be willing to follow on those promises even when this means you have to find compromises; the result is that in another life you'll be in a better position to pursue the work, having fulfilled your oaths.

replies.title: Re: Cold water purifications

comments.title: Magical ethics & raspberry jam principle
comments.content:
How do you judge whether a magical act is ethical and not subject to some sort of backlash? Are the rules different for non-magical acts?

For example, if I am trying to convince someone to give me a job, it's expected that one would use every means at one's disposal to not only get the job but negotiate the best salary and benefits. As well as the usual research and interview skills, I would probably be making use of any persuasion skills/NLP etc I could muster. Obviously "every means at one's disposal" does not include clearly illegal and unethical things like kidnapping the interviewer's family and holding them hostage until he or she agrees to give me what I want.

If I were to make a honey jar with some commanding herbs to influence the interviewer to give me the job/salary I desire, would this be subject to some sort of backlash or does it only apply to clearly unethical work like sending graveyars spirits to compel the interviewer with harsher work?

If influencing the interviewer would be subject to some sort of backlash because it is unethical, would the use of non-magical influencing like NLP/persuasion also be subject to some sort of negative rebound effect?

I've had enough TSW experiences that I am giving this some serious thought, but I think I am suffering some sort of analysis paralysis since I'm having a hard time seeing how any sort of magical work doesn't involve a degree of manipulation even if it not always direct - and I'm finding it hard to undetstand how one would achieve anything without trying to influence people or events. Any input would be very appreciated.

Thanks again for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: Magical ethics & raspberry jam principle
replies.content:
The Golden Rule is a good general guideline. Would you want someone else to try to control your actions in order to make you give them a job you may or may not deserve? If not, don't do it to someone else.

By and large, your best bet with magic is not to aim it at any person other than yourself. Focus your magic on making yourself more employable, more congenial, more lovable, a better worker and friend and lover, and people will line up to employ you, befriend you, and tumble into bed with you.

comments.title: Beloved Objects that Need to Go Now
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

I am not a practitioner of magic but I do have some feeling for the energies in things (psychometry) and am also sensitive to the distress that I and others oftentimes feel when faced with the need to discard certain things-- specifically, things with no practical purpose, no value to others, and no reasonable way to store, but that nonetheless have powerful emotional associations.

I have an attic to clear, filled with such things as my deceased mother's childhood teddybear-- which is bald, dusty, stuffing extruding, and altogether rather large. No one would want it, and that definitively includes me. Obviously this object needs to be disposed of. But this is just so difficult!! That sort of thing-- things that were indeed loved, and/or important to a beloved, but now they need be disposed of.

I am not talking about items for donation or resale, I am talking about things that really need to be added to the junk haul. Or, less elegantly put, in the garbage.

Some personal organizers recommend that one take a picture of the object, then "let it go." Well, no thanks, because I do not actually want to look at it. I want to dispose of it. And disposing of it is the hard part.

Marie Kondo the tidying expert (revealing some Shinto influence) says to thank the object, and then let it go-- and that sounds good to me--but somehow, for me (and I suspect for many other people as well) this is not enough. It feels to brutal to just toss such a thing into a garbage bag.

Is there something more, perhaps some simple ritual that would lend some dignity to disposing of such emotionally-charged but useless objects?

Thank you for considering my question.

MILLICENTLY LURKING

replies.content: First, I'd encourage you to journal about it, and make sure you've sorted through the feelings that surround the object. Then do as Marie Kondo suggests. I don't know of any particular ritual, and very often the simpler approach is better anyway.

ML

Otherwise, thank it for being a companion for your mother, place it in a dark garbage bag and toss it out!

replies.title: Re: Beloved Objects that Need to Go Now

replies.content:
I would set up a ritual space (banish/invoke as usual) present the object to the Four Quarters (plus Above and Below if that is your tradition) speaking its good qualities at each. Then, commune with it in the Center or Place of Spirit considering for a time the love and care it represents. Then say "As the body decays so dost thou decay. I honor thee, I thank thee, I release thee." I would then ask Kali or any other Destroyer figure (Angel Michael?) to sever the connection between material object and the spirit of love so the object can go to its well-earned rest.

Burn what you can in a fire. If a fire is not practical, then a gleeful, childlike "stuffing and stomping" fest as you put the divorced material objects into a cardboard box (NOT a plastic bag, please!) and put it outside for three days, exposed to all weathers. Once it has been exposed, it can be treated as any other trash or garbage for disposal. After the separation ceremony, a small meal celebrating the spirits of love and care (or a libation and placing of food for small critters) would afford a final seal of closure.

replies.title: Re: Heathen Golden Dawn
replies.content:
Runic divination seems to be a new thing, not much older than the 1980s. I like Edred Thorsson's early books, "Runelore" and "At the Well of Wyrd" on the subject of runic divination.

Almost all the old data we have to go on when we try to use runes for divination are the names of the letters themselves and the corresponding verses in the three extant rune poems (Anglo-Saxon, Norwegian and Icelandic). Intuition will have to fill in the gaps ...

Myself, I use the elder futhark, and my runesets are made of wood, not stone, as a matter of principle. (Runes were originally carved in wood; runes carved in stone are definitely a secondary and elite thing.)

The use of runes in magical work, however, goes way, way back, just as does the use of any other alphabet in magical work. As with other alphabets, so with runes: magical uses of the alphabet are definitely a secondary thing, not the reason for the alphabet's creation and existence. Since you can read Scandinavian languages, take a look at Anders Bæksted's "Malruner og troldruner" (1952) for a very sober and cautious look at all the available evidence on early rune magic.

In fairly recent centuries, as runic writing fell out of common use, runes acquired a sort of antiquarian glamour that lent itself to magic. There was definitely a fair amount of runic magic around in Scandinavia during the High Middle Ages and later, especially in Iceland.

comments.title: The Power of Belief
comments.content:
There seems to be a persistent line of thought in Modern Occultism, which states that belief is the sole basis for magic and that anything external to the magician is just a way to amplify that belief. As someone who studies Traditional Sources such as Agrippa or your eurdite Encyclopedia of Natural Magic, I find the Mental Model of magic, by itself, wanting.

It's obvious that belief, or more precisely the Will, is integral to magic after hours of study and practice, but it seems equally obvious that there are other wills and powers that are greater than the human will.

There are plenty of logical problems I can detect to, such as the underlying fact that the mind being immaterial and corresponding to the body, that it logically follows that there are other immaterial essences that correspond to other physical bodies that make up reality. Thus, just as the soul can act and correspondingly change reality, than these other essences can too, and the wise magician seeks them out and utilizes their power.

Why do you think that the Mental Model of Magic persists? I understand the historical process that spawned it, but I sense a psychological reason for why it's so alluring. Is it the vanity that Western Culture has about our species, which you have described so well, that makes so many Modern Magicians so smugly certain that the human mind is the most powerful source there is?

replies.title: Re: The Power of Belief
replies.content: Yes, as I see it, the attraction of the mental model is ordinary human vanity. It appeals to our overdeveloped sense of entitlement to think that the whole universe does what we tell it. The mere fact that this works so poorly in practice rarely gets a word in edgewise, and of course a fair number of the people who embrace this approach to magic have quite an impressive array of defensive strategies in place to ignore or explain away their many failures...

comments.title: Dion Fortune and Mayan Contacts
comments.content:
Dear JMG:

I have a question concerning something Dion Fortune states towards the end of Chapter XVII (Eastern Methods and Western Bodies) of Sane Occultism. She states that certain aspects of occult work are tied in to particular lands, and that while Americans can study Western esoteric philosophy and science they really should "seek the contacts of the Sun Temple of Atlantis through the Maya tradition. Egypt has no message for the United States." (Fortune, p. 168) on that same page she suggests that an American would revive the Mayan contacts and modify them to suit the initiatory needs of his civilization. How would you interpret all this? Thank you in advance for your time and consideration, it's really appreciated.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune and Mayan Contacts
replies.content:
Fortune never came to this side of the Atlantic, and I think she was mistaken. The Mayan tradition is one branch of the broader Mesoamerican tradition, which has its roots in the Olmec civilization before 1500 BCE -- long before the Mayans, that is -- and survived in various forms until the Spanish conquest. It's an option, especially for those who live in Mexico, the southwestern US, Central America and northern South America, the historic homelands of the tradition; a case could be made for the Mississippi valley as well, as Mexican traditions came north and shaped the Mound Builder cultures there.

Where I live in New England, by contrast, the geology is identical with western Britain -- we're part of the same ancient continent from before Pangaean times, which charmingly enough has been named Avalonia by geologists -- and the energies Fortune worked with function very well here; they also seem to function all along the Atlantic seaboard and west through the Appalachians, though in a more muted fashion. The Pacific Northwest, the other place where I've done magic extensively, has its own very distinct energies, which are akin to those you'll find all around the North Pacific arc -- for example, Japanese contacts are very easy to pick up there.

The short form to all this is that Fortune, like a lot of English people, never really grasped the sheer scale of the American land. The United States is bigger than western Europe, and like Europe, it contains many different countries; just as Europe has areas where the Celtic influence is strong, and other areas where Teutonic and Classical influences are stronger, the US is magically divided into different regions with their own magical energies.

replies.content:

comments.title: Needing some help with bullying
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I live in a small community and have been slandered by one woman a great deal. I don't mind this as much as the way she sucks up to me every time she sees me. We used to belong to the same painting group for some years and finally, she needled me enough to get the response she wanted. I screamed at her to stop being stupid. I left the group and never had anything to do with them again.

That said, we live in a small community and I sometimes run into one or the other of the group. It is always startlingly nasty. I am empathic and they are holding a grudge all out of proportion to what I did and it was four years ago. I suspect this is because they lost control of their youngest member.

I do not want to do a sweetening spell because I want nothing to do with them. Is there a spell to make them forget me or any spell that will get them off my case?

When I get home after these encounters, a Hoodoo wash and some Florida Water restores my good humor. I had forgotten to put on my protective charm this last time. I am making a fresh one.
Maxine

Maxine

comments.title: Intuition at work
comments.content:
Thank you for these Magic Mondays.

I seem to have increasing intuition and/or psychic ability of some sort...I have shared dreams with a dear friend, dreamed of an event that turned out to happen the next day with a staff member, felt my dog begging to be let out of his crate while I was at work, etc.

I also have developed a knack for asking exactly the right questions at work that get to the heart of underlying issues that are unknown to me when I ask the question. This is causing issues with my boss and a few colleagues who think I know more than I really do (consciously, at least), are uncomfortable with the truth the (seemingly to me) innocent question points to, or believe that I am actively meddling. For example, I asked a departing staff person in another area if we have a retention problem, believing the answer was "no." Staff person said no. Staff person's supervisor took me to task for making the person feel uncomfortable with a direct question. Later I learn that the departing staff person is leaving partly because of issues with her boss. I had no idea. But I'm the bad guy for provoking discomfort by a question.

My question is how to nurture this gift of different ways of knowing and how to continue my leadership role at work safely. I could simply stop asking questions that are outside my lane (which I intend to do anyway), but sometimes in meetings, questions just come up with little time to meditate on whether or not asking them will move the group forward. And if the question points to an unknown issue, how do I protect myself? Perhaps SoP and amulet? Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Intuition at work
replies.content: How to manage intuitive insights among people who don't understand magic is something everyone who does this work has to do sooner or later. Mostly it involves keeping one's mouth shut a lot, especially when it's outside your specific task or the like. Regular banishings are certainly useful, but discretion is at least as important...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm interested in cutting my internet use, and one avenue I came up with was to look into the shady side of it, with the idea being that if I knew what it was for in more depth, I'd want to use it less. As a result, I picked up Surveillance Valley, and must say it's a wonderful book. It describes the history of the internet, and how it has been a surveillance and control system from the start (back in the 1960s). Interestingly, people in the 1960s and 1970s fought against it, and pushed for all sorts of reforms to hold ARPANET back, and they were justified to think computers were dangerous for democracy and civil rights.

Everything in it is thoroughly researched and very well written, but this raises a huge question: how on Earth did the techno-utopian ideas form in the 1980s, and how can they still thrive today? One of my personal favorite bits of the book is that TOR is funded by the US government, and yet all the privacy wonks say it's great, and that of course it can be used to avoid surveillance and... There's something really weird going on here. What sort of things can cause a mass delusion on this scale?

How do we avoid being drawn in? Since this seems like a powerful current, whatever it is, I suspect it influences nearly all of us to some extent or other. I assume we just need to let it run its course, but it seems fairly freaky that so many people are placing utopian fantasies on a spy network....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's easier to daydream about utopia than it is to deal with the fact that you're a cog in a vast corporate structure that has no interest at all in your well-being. People love to delude themselves...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It seems totally crazy to me though that the daydreams about utopia are based around the heart of the vast corporate structure. Id have expected the dreams of utopia to be spread out more, so I suppose what I'm asking is if you know what is it about the internet that draws utopian fantasies onto it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Because it's so totally unreal; you can project any emotional pattern onto it, and since it's all just arbitrary images on a screen, maintaining the projection is easy.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: At a personal level then, how do we avoid falling victim to it? Is it a matter of meditation, a banishing ritual, and time, or are there other ways to keep it from having too much of an impact?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Don't spend more time on it than you have to. Yes, banishing rituals and meditation also help.

comments.title: Erik Satie
comments.content:
Upon his death, there were found in Satie's apartment 150 black suits, such as he wore every day, and two grand pianos, one upside down upon the other. He like to be called "Mr. Poverty."

I love his music for its total absence of egoic aggrandizement, the diametric opposite of Wagner and many others.
